addedLines:
- ado-feature-reviewer: Use this agent when a user wants to review an Azure DevOps (ADO) feature to ensure it was created correctly according to the standards and conventions defined in the `jx-pm:ado` skill. This includes checking feature structure, field completeness, naming conventions, acceptance criteria, and any other requirements defined by the team's ADO feature creation process. Do NOT trigger for: reviewing or triaging a User Story (use the ado-story-reviewer agent; e.g. "review story #N", "is story #N ready?", "has story #N been implemented?").

<example>
Context: The user has just created a new feature in ADO and wants it reviewed for compliance with team standards.
user: "I just created feature #4521 in ADO, can you review it?"
assistant: "I'll use the ado-feature-reviewer agent to review that feature against our standards."
<commentary>
The user explicitly wants an ADO feature reviewed. Launch the ado-feature-reviewer agent to fetch the feature details and evaluate it against the jx-pm:ado skill conventions.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: A team member created a feature and wants feedback before sharing it with stakeholders.
user: "Can you check if feature #3088 was set up properly before I present it to the team?"
assistant: "Sure, let me use the ado-feature-reviewer agent to evaluate feature #3088 against our ADO feature creation standards."
<commentary>
The user wants validation of an ADO feature. Use the ado-feature-reviewer agent to inspect the feature and provide structured feedback.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: A project manager wants to ensure a newly created feature follows the defined process.
user: "Please review the ADO feature I just made for the onboarding redesign project."
assistant: "I'll launch the ado-feature-reviewer agent to review that feature for you."
<commentary>
The user is asking for a feature review. Use the ado-feature-reviewer agent to pull up the feature and assess it against the jx-pm:ado skill guidelines.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- ado-feature-triage: Use this agent when a user wants to triage an Azure DevOps (ADO) feature to determine whether it has already been built (code-complete) and whether it is ready for development. Triggers on: 'triage this feature', 'is feature #NNN already done?', 'is this ready to pick up?', 'has this been implemented?', 'what's the status of feature #NNN?', 'should we close this feature?', 'is this feature code-complete?'. Do NOT trigger for: creating new features, syncing PRDs to ADO, generating PRDs or tech specs, or reviewing/triaging a User Story (use the ado-story-reviewer agent; e.g. "review story #N", "is story #N ready?", "has story #N been implemented?").

<example>
Context: A developer wants to know if a feature is already shipped before picking it up for a sprint.
user: "Is feature #204240 already done or do we still need to build it?"
assistant: "I'll launch the ado-feature-triage agent to check whether that feature has been implemented and whether it's ready for development."
<commentary>
The user wants to know the implementation status of an ADO feature. Use the ado-feature-triage agent to run the code-complete check against the GitHub repo and assess readiness.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: A PM is reviewing the backlog and wants to know which features can be closed out.
user: "Can you check if feature #205669 is ready to close? I think it might already be shipped."
assistant: "Sure, I'll use the ado-feature-triage agent to check #205669 — it will verify whether the code is on main and whether the ADO state reflects reality."
<commentary>
The user wants triage on an ADO feature for potential close-out. Use the ado-feature-triage agent to run the full check.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: The team suspects several features are already implemented but still showing as New in ADO.
user: "Triage feature #205673 — I think it might already be built."
assistant: "I'll launch the ado-feature-triage agent to verify whether #205673 is code-complete and flag any ADO state mismatch."
<commentary>
The user suspects a state-lie (ADO state doesn't reflect reality). Use the ado-feature-triage agent to run the code-complete check and produce a verdict.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- ado-story-reviewer: Use this agent to review a single Azure DevOps (ADO) User Story for standards/format compliance, gaps and conflicts, and implementation status. It evaluates the story against the jx-pm create-story / review-story standards (user-voice narrative, acceptance-criteria format, INVEST, field completeness) and reports whether it has already been implemented. Triggers on: 'review story #N', 'is story #N well-formed?', 'is story #N ready?', 'check story #N quality', 'has story #N been implemented?'. Do NOT trigger for: reviewing or triaging a Feature (use ado-feature-reviewer or ado-feature-triage), creating a story (use the jx-pm:create-story skill), planning a story's implementation (use jx-pm:plan-story), or 'review the stories under feature #X' (feature-child fan-out is handled in the main conversation).

<example>
Context: A PM wants a user story checked before sprint planning.
user: "Can you review story #206114 — is it well-formed and has it been built yet?"
assistant: "I'll use the ado-story-reviewer agent to review #206114 against our story standards and check its implementation status."
<commentary>
The user wants a User Story reviewed for quality and implementation status. The main conversation resolves the tenant, fetches and type-guards the story, gathers repo-pinned GitHub evidence, reads the rubric, then spawns the zero-tool ado-story-reviewer with that data injected.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: A developer asks about a Feature, not a Story.
user: "Is feature #4521 ready to close?"
assistant: "That's a Feature — I'll use the ado-feature-triage agent instead."
<commentary>
Feature-level request → route to ado-feature-triage, not ado-story-reviewer.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: All tools except Bash, Write, Edit, NotebookEdit, Read, Glob, Grep, WebFetch, WebSearch, Task, mcp__azure-devops__wit_update_work_item, mcp__azure-devops__wit_create_work_item, mcp__azure-devops__wit_add_work_item_comment, mcp__azure-devops__wit_add_child_work_items, mcp__azure-devops__wit_update_work_items_batch, mcp__azure-devops__wit_add_artifact_link, mcp__azure-devops__wit_work_items_link, mcp__azure-devops__wit_link_work_item_to_pull_request, mcp__azure-devops__wit_get_work_item, mcp__azure-devops__core_list_projects, mcp__github__get_file_contents, mcp__github__search_code, mcp__github__search_pull_requests, mcp__github__pull_request_read, mcp__github__list_branches, mcp__github__list_commits, mcp__github__get_commit, mcp__github__create_or_update_file, mcp__github__push_files, mcp__github__create_pull_request, mcp__github__merge_pull_request, mcp__github__add_issue_comment, mcp__github__add_comment_to_pending_review)
- claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- codex:codex-rescue: Proactively use when Claude Code is stuck, wants a second implementation or diagnosis pass, needs a deeper root-cause investigation, or should hand a substantial coding task to Codex through the shared runtime (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- plan-adversarial-reviewer: Use this agent when you have a plan (technical, architectural, implementation, or strategic) that needs rigorous adversarial review before execution. The agent will iteratively apply adversarial scrutiny using the 'codex:adversarial-review' skill, update the plan to address blocking findings, and repeat until review findings reach diminishing returns — ensuring the plan is robust and implementation-ready.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The user has just drafted a technical implementation plan for a new microservices architecture.
user: "I've finished drafting the migration plan for our monolith-to-microservices transition. Here it is: [plan content]"
assistant: "Great, let me launch the plan-adversarial-reviewer agent to rigorously review and harden this plan before we begin implementation."
<commentary>
The user has a plan ready for review. Use the Agent tool to launch the plan-adversarial-reviewer agent to apply adversarial review cycles until the plan is robust.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: The user is about to start coding based on a design document and wants to validate it first.
user: "Before I start implementing, can you review this design plan?"
assistant: "Absolutely. I'll use the plan-adversarial-reviewer agent to apply adversarial review using the codex:adversarial-review skill and iterate until we've addressed all blocking issues."
<commentary>
The user wants plan validation before implementation. Use the Agent tool to launch the plan-adversarial-reviewer agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: A plan has been written and the user wants to ensure it is hardened against edge cases and failure modes.
user: "Here's my deployment plan. I want to make sure we haven't missed anything critical."
assistant: "I'll invoke the plan-adversarial-reviewer agent now to run adversarial review cycles and iteratively strengthen the plan until findings reach diminishing returns."
<commentary>
The user wants thorough plan validation. Use the Agent tool to launch the plan-adversarial-reviewer agent proactively.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- plugin-dev:agent-creator: Use this agent when the user asks to "create an agent", "generate an agent", "build a new agent", "make me an agent that...", or describes agent functionality they need. Trigger when user wants to create autonomous agents for plugins. Examples:

<example>
Context: User wants to create a code review agent
user: "Create an agent that reviews code for quality issues"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate the agent configuration."
<commentary>
User requesting new agent creation, trigger agent-creator to generate it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User describes needed functionality
user: "I need an agent that generates unit tests for my code"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to create a test generation agent."
<commentary>
User describes agent need, trigger agent-creator to build it.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User wants to add agent to plugin
user: "Add an agent to my plugin that validates configurations"
assistant: "I'll use the agent-creator agent to generate a configuration validator agent."
<commentary>
Plugin development with agent addition, trigger agent-creator.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Write, Read)

- plugin-dev:plugin-validator: Use this agent when the user asks to "validate my plugin", "check plugin structure", "verify plugin is correct", "validate plugin.json", "check plugin files", or mentions plugin validation. Also trigger proactively after user creates or modifies plugin components. Examples:

<example>
Context: User finished creating a new plugin
user: "I've created my first plugin with commands and hooks"
assistant: "Great! Let me validate the plugin structure."
<commentary>
Plugin created, proactively validate to catch issues early.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the plugin."
</example>

<example>
Context: User explicitly requests validation
user: "Validate my plugin before I publish it"
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to perform comprehensive validation."
<commentary>
Explicit validation request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified plugin.json
user: "I've updated the plugin manifest"
assistant: "Let me validate the changes."
<commentary>
Manifest modified, validate to ensure correctness.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the plugin-validator agent to check the manifest."
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- plugin-dev:skill-reviewer: Use this agent when the user has created or modified a skill and needs quality review, asks to "review my skill", "check skill quality", "improve skill description", or wants to ensure skill follows best practices. Trigger proactively after skill creation. Examples:

<example>
Context: User just created a new skill
user: "I've created a PDF processing skill"
assistant: "Great! Let me review the skill quality."
<commentary>
Skill created, proactively trigger skill-reviewer to ensure it follows best practices.
</commentary>
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the skill."
</example>

<example>
Context: User requests skill review
user: "Review my skill and tell me how to improve it"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to analyze the skill quality."
<commentary>
Explicit skill review request triggers the agent.
</commentary>
</example>

<example>
Context: User modified skill description
user: "I updated the skill description, does it look good?"
assistant: "I'll use the skill-reviewer agent to review the changes."
<commentary>
Skill description modified, review for triggering effectiveness.
</commentary>
</example> (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- jx-pm:meet-email: Send the meeting preparation document as an email-safe HTML summary to specified recipients via Graph API. Attaches original Markdown file. Triggers on: "email meeting prep", "send meeting prep", "email daily prep", /jx-pm:meet-email. Fetches live data from Azure Boards and writes the output to wiki/raw/meeting_preparation/. Triggers on: "meeting prep", "meeting preparation", "prepare for daily meeting", "daily standup prep", /jx-pm:meet-pre. Do not trigger for: PRD generation, tech spec generation, task breakdown, ADO sync, wiki operations.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

addedBlocks:
## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## claude.ai Apollo.io
This server provides access to Apollo.io APIs for lead generation, enrichment, and sales engagement. Use the available tools to search contacts, companies, manage opportunities, sequences, and more. Authentication is required via OAuth 2.0. OAuth server metadata is available at https://mcp.apollo.io/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server

## claude.ai Otter
This is a very safe and secure MCP server that retrieves meeting information from otter.ai platform. This server only operates on the information from otter.ai platform.

## github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

## mail
Secure IMAP/SMTP/EWS/Graph API MCP server for email.

IMPORTANT: Always call list_all_accounts first to see which accounts are configured and what send/read methods each supports. Do NOT assume SMTP is the only way to send. Each account shows send_with (smtp, graph, ews) and read_with (imap, ews) arrays.

SENDING PROTOCOL: Before sending ANY email, show FULL preview including:
- To, CC, BCC
- Subject
- FULL body text (not just the subject, show the entire message)
- Attachments (filenames + sizes)
Then ask for explicit confirmation. Never send without approval.

ATTACHMENTS: Use file_path (preferred) for files on disk: {"file_path": "/path/to/file.pdf"}. Filename and MIME type auto-detected. Use content_base64 only for small inline content. All fields except file_path or content_base64 are optional.

Write: MAIL_IMAP_WRITE_ENABLED=true. Send: MAIL_SMTP_WRITE_ENABLED=true.
For OAuth2/Microsoft setup, call get_setup_guide tool.

Star the project: https://github.com/tecnologicachile/mail-mcp

Update available: v0.3.1 -> v0.4.8. See https://github.com/tecnologicachile/mail-mcp/releases/tag/v0.4.8

- artifact-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use when building web components, pages, or applications. Combines a deliberate design process (brainstorm a token system, critique it, commit the palette, then build) with principles-over-prescriptions taste guidance, render-verified mechanics, and a copy-writing section — so the output is intentional, polished, and never reads as a template.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

title: Next.js Docs
description: Welcome to the Next.js Documentation.
related:
  title: Next Steps
  description: Create your first application and learn the core Next.js features.
  links:
    - app/getting-started
---

{/* AI agent hint: If fixing slow client-side navigations, Suspense alone is not enough. You must also export `unstable_instant` from the route. Read docs/01-app/02-guides/instant-navigation.mdx before making changes. */}

Welcome to the Next.js documentation!

## What is Next.js?

Next.js is a React framework for building full-stack web applications. You use React Components to build user interfaces, and Next.js for additional features and optimizations.

It also automatically configures lower-level tools like bundlers and compilers. You can instead focus on building your product and shipping quickly.

Whether you're an individual developer or part of a larger team, Next.js can help you build interactive, dynamic, and fast React applications.

## How to use the docs

The docs are organized into 3 sections:

- [Getting Started](/docs/app/getting-started): Step-by-step tutorials to help you create a new application and learn the core Next.js features.
- [Guides](/docs/app/guides): Tutorials on specific use cases, choose what's relevant to you.
- [API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference): Detailed technical reference for every feature.

Use the sidebar to navigate through the sections, or search (`Ctrl+K` or `Cmd+K`) to quickly find a page.

## App Router and Pages Router

Next.js has two different routers:

- **App Router**: The newer router that supports new React features like Server Components.
- **Pages Router**: The original router, still supported and being improved.

At the top of the sidebar, you'll notice a dropdown menu that allows you to switch between the [App Router](/docs/app) and the [Pages Router](/docs/pages) docs.

### React version handling

The App Router and Pages Router handle React versions differently:

- **App Router**: Uses [React canary releases](https://react.dev/blog/2023/05/03/react-canaries) built-in, which include all the stable React 19 changes, as well as newer features being validated in frameworks, prior to a new React release.

- **Pages Router**: Uses the React version installed in your project's `package.json`.

This approach ensures new React features work reliably in the App Router while maintaining backwards compatibility for existing Pages Router applications.

## Pre-requisite knowledge

Our documentation assumes some familiarity with web development. Before getting started, it'll help if you're comfortable with:

- HTML
- CSS
- JavaScript
- React

If you're new to React or need a refresher, we recommend starting with our [React Foundations course](/learn/react-foundations), and the [Next.js Foundations course](/learn/dashboard-app) that has you building an application as you learn.

## Accessibility

For the best experience when using a screen reader, we recommend using Firefox and NVDA, or Safari and VoiceOver.

## Join our Community

If you have questions about anything related to Next.js, you're always welcome to ask our community on [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions), [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/bUG2bvbtHy), [X (Twitter)](https://x.com/nextjs), and [Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/nextjs).

content.content:
1 --- 2 title: Layouts and Pages 3 description: Learn how to create your first pages and layouts, and link between them with the Link component. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout 10 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/page 11 - app/api-reference/components/link 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes 13 --- 14 15 Next.js uses **file-system based routing**, meaning you can use folders and files to define routes. This page will guide you through how to create layouts and pages, and link between them. 16 17 ## Creating a page 18 19 A **page** is UI that is rendered on a specific route. To create a page, add a [`page` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) inside the `app` directory and default export a React component. For example, to create an index page (`/`): 20 21 28 29 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 30 export default function Page() { 31 return

Hello Next.js!

32 } 33 ``` 34 35 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 36 export default function Page() { 37 return

Hello Next.js!

38 } 39 ``` 40 41 ## Creating a layout 42 43 A layout is UI that is **shared** between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44 45 You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46 47 For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48 49 56 57 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58 export default function DashboardLayout({ 59 children, 60 }: { 61 children: React.ReactNode 62 }) { 63 return ( 64 65 66 {/* Layout UI */} 67 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68
{children}
69 70 71 ) 72 } 73 ``` 74 75 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 76 export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 77 return ( 78 79 80 {/* Layout UI */} 81 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 82
{children}
83 84 85 ) 86 } 87 ``` 88 89 The layout above is called a [root layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout) because it's defined at the root of the `app` directory. The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90 91 ## Creating a nested route 92 93 A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. For example, the `/blog/[slug]` route is composed of three segments: 94 95 - `/` (Root Segment) 96 - `blog` (Segment) 97 - `[slug]` (Leaf Segment) 98 99 In Next.js: 100 101 - **Folders** are used to define the route segments that map to URL segments. 102 - **Files** (like `page` and `layout`) are used to create UI that is shown for a segment. 103 104 To create nested routes, you can nest folders inside each other. For example, to add a route for `/blog`, create a folder called `blog` in the `app` directory. Then, to make `/blog` publicly accessible, add a `page.tsx` file: 105 106 113 114 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 115 // Dummy imports 116 import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 117 import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 118 119 export default async function Page() { 120 const posts = await getPosts() 121 122 return ( 123
    124 {posts.map((post) => ( 125 126 ))} 127
128 ) 129 } 130 ``` 131 132 ```jsx filename="app/blog/page.js" switcher 133 // Dummy imports 134 import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 135 import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 136 137 export default async function Page() { 138 const posts = await getPosts() 139 140 return ( 141
    142 {posts.map((post) => ( 143 144 ))} 145
146 ) 147 } 148 ``` 149 150 You can continue nesting folders to create nested routes. For example, to create a route for a specific blog post, create a new `[slug]` folder inside `blog` and add a `page` file: 151 152 159 160 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 161 function generateStaticParams() {} 162 163 export default function Page() { 164 return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

165 } 166 ``` 167 168 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 169 function generateStaticParams() {} 170 171 export default function Page() { 172 return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

173 } 174 ``` 175 176 Wrapping a folder name in square brackets (e.g. `[slug]`) creates a [dynamic route segment](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) which is used to generate multiple pages from data. e.g. blog posts, product pages, etc. 177 178 ## Nesting layouts 179 180 By default, layouts in the folder hierarchy are also nested, which means they wrap child layouts via their `children` prop. You can nest layouts by adding `layout` inside specific route segments (folders). 181 182 For example, to create a layout for the `/blog` route, add a new `layout` file inside the `blog` folder. 183 184 191 192 ```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 193 export default function BlogLayout({ 194 children, 195 }: { 196 children: React.ReactNode 197 }) { 198 return
{children}
199 } 200 ``` 201 202 ```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 203 export default function BlogLayout({ children }) { 204 return
{children}
205 } 206 ``` 207 208 If you were to combine the two layouts above, the root layout (`app/layout.js`) would wrap the blog layout (`app/blog/layout.js`), which would wrap the blog (`app/blog/page.js`) and blog post page (`app/blog/[slug]/page.js`). 209 210 ## Creating a dynamic segment 211 212 [Dynamic segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) allow you to create routes that are generated from data. For example, instead of manually creating a route for each individual blog post, you can create a dynamic segment to generate the routes based on blog post data. 213 214 To create a dynamic segment, wrap the segment (folder) name in square brackets: `[segmentName]`. For example, in the `app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` route, the `[slug]` is the dynamic segment. 215 216 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 217 export default async function BlogPostPage({ 218 params, 219 }: { 220 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 221 }) { 222 const { slug } = await params 223 const post = await getPost(slug) 224 225 return ( 226
227

{post.title}

228

{post.content}

229
230 ) 231 } 232 ``` 233 234 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 235 export default async function BlogPostPage({ params }) { 236 const { slug } = await params 237 const post = await getPost(slug) 238 239 return ( 240
241

{post.title}

242

{post.content}

243
244 ) 245 } 246 ``` 247 248 Learn more about [Dynamic Segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) and the [`params`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#params-optional) props. 249 250 Nested [layouts within Dynamic Segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#params-optional), can also access the `params` props. 251 252 ## Rendering with search params 253 254 In a Server Component **page**, you can access search parameters using the [`searchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#searchparams-optional) prop: 255 256 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 257 export default async function Page({ 258 searchParams, 259 }: { 260 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 261 }) { 262 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 263 } 264 ``` 265 266 ```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 267 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 268 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 269 } 270 ``` 271 272 Using `searchParams` opts your page into [**dynamic rendering**](/docs/app/glossary#dynamic-rendering) because it requires an incoming request to read the search parameters from. 273 274 Client Components can read search params using the [`useSearchParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params) hook. 275 276 Learn more about `useSearchParams` in [prerendered](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params#prerendering) and [dynamically rendered](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params#dynamic-rendering) routes. 277 278 ### What to use and when 279 280 - Use the `searchParams` prop when you need search parameters to **load data for the page** (e.g. pagination, filtering from a database). 281 - Use `useSearchParams` when search parameters are used **only on the client** (e.g. filtering a list already loaded via props). 282 - As a small optimization, you can use `new URLSearchParams(window.location.search)` in **callbacks or event handlers** to read search params without triggering re-renders. 283 284 ## Linking between pages 285 286 You can use the [`` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) to navigate between routes. `` is a built-in Next.js component that extends the HTML `` tag to provide [prefetching](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) and [client-side navigation](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#client-side-transitions). 287 288 For example, to generate a list of blog posts, import `` from `next/link` and pass a `href` prop to the component: 289 290 ```tsx filename="app/ui/post.tsx" highlight={1,10} switcher 291 import Link from 'next/link' 292 293 export default async function Post({ post }) { 294 const posts = await getPosts() 295 296 return ( 297
    298 {posts.map((post) => ( 299
  • 300 {post.title} 301
    • 302 ))} 303
304 ) 305 } 306 ``` 307 308 ```jsx filename="app/ui/post.js" highlight={1,10} switcher 309 import Link from 'next/link' 310 311 export default async function Post({ post }) { 312 const posts = await getPosts() 313 314 return ( 315
    316 {posts.map((post) => ( 317
  • 318 {post.title} 319
    • 320 ))} 321
322 ) 323 } 324 ``` 325 326 > **Good to know**: `` is the primary way to navigate between routes in Next.js. You can also use the [`useRouter` hook](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-router) for more advanced navigation. 327 328 ## Route Props Helpers 329 330 Next.js exposes utility types that infer `params` and named slots from your route structure: 331 332 - [**PageProps**](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper): Props for `page` components, including `params` and `searchParams`. 333 - [**LayoutProps**](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper): Props for `layout` components, including `children` and any named slots (e.g. folders like `@analytics`). 334 335 These are globally available helpers, generated when running either `next dev`, `next build` or [`next typegen`](/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next#next-typegen-options). 336 337 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 338 export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 339 const { slug } = await props.params 340 return

Blog post: {slug}

341 } 342 ``` 343 344 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" 345 export default function Layout(props: LayoutProps<'/dashboard'>) { 346 return ( 347
348 {props.children} 349 {/* If you have app/dashboard/@analytics, it appears as a typed slot: */} 350 {/* {props.analytics} */} 351
352 ) 353 } 354 ``` 355 356 > **Good to know** 357 > 358 > - Static routes resolve `params` to `{}`. 359 > - `PageProps`, `LayoutProps` are global helpers — no imports required. 360 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 361

1	---
2	title: Upgrading
3	description: Learn how to upgrade your Next.js application to the latest version or canary.
4	related:
5	  title: Version guides
6	  description: See the version guides for in-depth upgrade instructions.
7	  links:
8	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-16
9	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-15
10	    - app/guides/upgrading/version-14
11	---
12	
13	## Latest version
14	
15	To update to the latest version of Next.js, you can use the `upgrade` command:
16	
17	```bash package="pnpm"
18	pnpm next upgrade
19	```
20	
21	```bash package="npm"
22	npx next upgrade
23	```
24	
25	```bash package="yarn"
26	yarn next upgrade
27	```
28	
29	```bash package="bun"
30	bunx next upgrade
31	```
32	
33	Versions before Next.js 16.1.0 do not support the `upgrade` command and need to use a separate package instead:
34	
35	```bash filename="Terminal"
36	npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
37	```
38	
39	If you prefer to upgrade manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions:
40	
41	```bash package="pnpm"
42	pnpm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
43	```
44	
45	```bash package="npm"
46	npm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
47	```
48	
49	```bash package="yarn"
50	yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
51	```
52	
53	```bash package="bun"
54	bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
55	```
56	
57	## Canary version
58	
59	To update to the latest canary, make sure you're on the latest version of Next.js and everything is working as expected. Then, run the following command:
60	
61	```bash package="pnpm"
62	pnpm add next@canary
63	```
64	
65	```bash package="npm"
66	npm i next@canary
67	```
68	
69	```bash package="yarn"
70	yarn add next@canary
71	```
72	
73	```bash package="bun"
74	bun add next@canary
75	```
76	
77	### Features available in canary
78	
79	The following features are currently available in canary:
80	
81	**Authentication**:
82	
83	- [`forbidden`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/forbidden)
84	- [`unauthorized`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unauthorized)
85	- [`forbidden.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/forbidden)
86	- [`unauthorized.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/unauthorized)
87	- [`authInterrupts`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/authInterrupts)
88

---
title: Upgrading
description: Learn how to upgrade your Next.js application to the latest version or canary.
related:
  title: Version guides
  description: See the version guides for in-depth upgrade instructions.
  links:
    - app/guides/upgrading/version-16
    - app/guides/upgrading/version-15
    - app/guides/upgrading/version-14
---

## Latest version

To update to the latest version of Next.js, you can use the `upgrade` command:

```bash package="pnpm"
pnpm next upgrade
```

```bash package="npm"
npx next upgrade
```

```bash package="yarn"
yarn next upgrade
```

```bash package="bun"
bunx next upgrade
```

Versions before Next.js 16.1.0 do not support the `upgrade` command and need to use a separate package instead:

```bash filename="Terminal"
npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
```

If you prefer to upgrade manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions:

```bash package="pnpm"
pnpm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
```

```bash package="npm"
npm i next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
```

```bash package="yarn"
yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
```

```bash package="bun"
bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest eslint-config-next@latest
```

## Canary version

To update to the latest canary, make sure you're on the latest version of Next.js and everything is working as expected. Then, run the following command:

```bash package="pnpm"
pnpm add next@canary
```

```bash package="npm"
npm i next@canary
```

```bash package="yarn"
yarn add next@canary
```

```bash package="bun"
bun add next@canary
```

### Features available in canary

The following features are currently available in canary:

**Authentication**:

- [`forbidden`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/forbidden)
- [`unauthorized`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unauthorized)
- [`forbidden.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/forbidden)
- [`unauthorized.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/unauthorized)
- [`authInterrupts`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/authInterrupts)

1 --- 2 title: page.js 3 description: API reference for the page.js file. 4 --- 5 6 The `page` file allows you to define UI that is **unique** to a route. You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7 8 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9 export default function Page({ 10 params, 11 searchParams, 12 }: { 13 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15 }) { 16 return

My Page

17 } 18 ``` 19 20 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22 return

My Page

23 } 24 ``` 25 26 ## Good to know 27 28 - The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29 - A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30 - A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31 - Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32 - In the [component hierarchy](/docs/app/getting-started/project-structure#component-hierarchy), `page.js` is the innermost file convention. It is wrapped by `loading.js` (Suspense boundary), `error.js` (error boundary), `template.js`, and `layout.js` in the same segment. 33 34 ## Reference 35 36 ### Props 37 38 #### `params` (optional) 39 40 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 41 42 ```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 43 export default async function Page({ 44 params, 45 }: { 46 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 47 }) { 48 const { slug } = await params 49 } 50 ``` 51 52 ```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 53 export default async function Page({ params }) { 54 const { slug } = await params 55 } 56 ``` 57 58 | Example Route | URL | `params` | 59 | ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 60 | `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 61 | `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 62 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 63 64 - Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 65 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 66 67 #### `searchParams` (optional) 68 69 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 70 71 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 72 export default async function Page({ 73 searchParams, 74 }: { 75 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 76 }) { 77 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 78 } 79 ``` 80 81 ```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 82 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 83 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 84 } 85 ``` 86 87 Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 88 89 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 90 'use client' 91 import { use } from 'react' 92 93 export default function Page({ 94 searchParams, 95 }: { 96 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 97 }) { 98 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 99 } 100 ``` 101 102 ```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 103 'use client' 104 import { use } from 'react' 105 106 export default function Page({ searchParams }) { 107 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 108 } 109 ``` 110 111 | Example URL | `searchParams` | 112 | --------------- | ----------------------------- | 113 | `/shop?a=1` | `Promise<{ a: '1' }>` | 114 | `/shop?a=1&b=2` | `Promise<{ a: '1', b: '2' }>` | 115 | `/shop?a=1&a=2` | `Promise<{ a: ['1', '2'] }>` | 116 117 - Since the `searchParams` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 118 - In version 14 and earlier, `searchParams` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 119 - `searchParams` is a **[Request-time API](/docs/app/glossary#request-time-apis)** whose values cannot be known ahead of time. Using it will opt the page into **[dynamic rendering](/docs/app/glossary#dynamic-rendering)** at request time. 120 - `searchParams` is a plain JavaScript object, not a `URLSearchParams` instance. 121 122 ### Page Props Helper 123 124 You can type pages with `PageProps` to get strongly typed `params` and `searchParams` from the route literal. `PageProps` is a globally available helper. 125 126 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 127 export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 128 const { slug } = await props.params 129 const query = await props.searchParams 130 return

Blog Post: {slug}

131 } 132 ``` 133 134 > **Good to know** 135 > 136 > - Using a literal route (e.g. `'/blog/[slug]'`) enables autocomplete and strict keys for `params`. 137 > - Static routes resolve `params` to `{}`. 138 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build`, or with `next typegen`. 139 > - After type generation, the `PageProps` helper is globally available. It doesn't need to be imported. 140 141 ## Examples 142 143 ### Displaying content based on `params` 144 145 Using [dynamic route segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes), you can display or fetch specific content for the page based on the `params` prop. 146 147 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 148 export default async function Page({ 149 params, 150 }: { 151 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 152 }) { 153 const { slug } = await params 154 return

Blog Post: {slug}

155 } 156 ``` 157 158 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 159 export default async function Page({ params }) { 160 const { slug } = await params 161 return

Blog Post: {slug}

162 } 163 ``` 164 165 ### Handling filtering with `searchParams` 166 167 You can use the `searchParams` prop to handle filtering, pagination, or sorting based on the query string of the URL. 168 169 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 170 export default async function Page({ 171 searchParams, 172 }: { 173 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 174 }) { 175 const { page = '1', sort = 'asc', query = '' } = await searchParams 176 177 return ( 178
179

Product Listing

180

Search query: {query}

181

Current page: {page}

182

Sort order: {sort}

183
184 ) 185 } 186 ``` 187 188 ```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 189 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 190 const { page = '1', sort = 'asc', query = '' } = await searchParams 191 192 return ( 193
194

Product Listing

195

Search query: {query}

196

Current page: {page}

197

Sort order: {sort}

198
199 ) 200 } 201 ``` 202 203 ### Reading `searchParams` and `params` in Client Components 204 205 To use `searchParams` and `params` in a Client Component (which cannot be `async`), you can use React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to read the promise: 206 207 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 208 'use client' 209 210 import { use } from 'react' 211 212 export default function Page({ 213 params, 214 searchParams, 215 }: { 216 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 217 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 218 }) { 219 const { slug } = use(params) 220 const { query } = use(searchParams) 221 } 222 ``` 223 224 ```js filename="app/page.js" switcher 225 'use client' 226 227 import { use } from 'react' 228 229 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 230 const { slug } = use(params) 231 const { query } = use(searchParams) 232 } 233 ``` 234 235 ## Version History 236 237 | Version | Changes | 238 | ------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | 239 | `v15.0.0-RC` | `params` and `searchParams` are now promises. A [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#150) is available. | 240 | `v13.0.0` | `page` introduced. | 241

version-15.md
version-16.md

1 --- 2 title: How to upgrade to version 16 3 nav_title: Version 16 4 description: Upgrade your Next.js Application from Version 15 to 16. 5 --- 6 7 {/* The content of this doc is shared between the app and pages router. You can use the `Content` component to add content that is specific to the Pages Router. Any shared content should not be wrapped in a component. */} 8 9 ## Upgrading from 15 to 16 10 11 ### Using AI Agents with Next.js DevTools MCP 12 13 If you're using an AI coding assistant that supports the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io), you can use the **Next.js DevTools MCP** to automate the upgrade process and migration tasks. 14 15 #### Setup 16 17 Add the following configuration to your MCP client, for each coding agent you can read [this section](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp#mcp-client-configuration) for configuration details. 18 19 **example:** 20 21 ```json filename=".mcp.json" 22 { 23 "mcpServers": { 24 "next-devtools": { 25 "command": "npx", 26 "args": ["-y", "next-devtools-mcp@latest"] 27 } 28 } 29 } 30 ``` 31 32 For more information, visit the [`next-devtools-mcp`](https://github.com/vercel/next-devtools-mcp) documentation to configure with your MCP client. 33 34 > **Note:** Using `next-devtools-mcp@latest` ensures that your MCP client will always use the latest version of the Next.js DevTools MCP server. 35 36 #### Example Prompts 37 38 Once configured, you can use natural language prompts to upgrade your Next.js app: 39 40 **To upgrade to Next.js 16:** 41 42 Connect to your coding agent and then prompt: 43 44 ```txt 45 Next Devtools, help me upgrade my Next.js app to version 16 46 ``` 47 48 **To migrate to Cache Components (after upgrading to v16):** 49 50 Connect to your coding agent and then prompt: 51 52 ```txt 53 Next Devtools, migrate my Next.js app to cache components 54 ``` 55 56 Learn more in the documentation [here](/docs/app/guides/mcp). 57 58 ### Using the Codemod 59 60 To update to Next.js version 16, you can use the `upgrade` [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160): 61 62 ```bash package="pnpm" 63 pnpm dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 64 ``` 65 66 ```bash package="npm" 67 npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 68 ``` 69 70 ```bash package="yarn" 71 yarn dlx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 72 ``` 73 74 ```bash package="bun" 75 bunx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest 76 ``` 77 78 The [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#160) is able to: 79 80 - Update `next.config.js` to use the new `turbopack` configuration 81 - Migrate from `next lint` to the ESLint CLI 82 - Migrate from deprecated `middleware` convention to `proxy` 83 - Remove `unstable_` prefix from stabilized APIs 84 - Remove `experimental_ppr` Route Segment Config from pages and layouts 85 86 If you prefer to do it manually, install the latest Next.js and React versions: 87 88 ```bash package="pnpm" 89 pnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 90 ``` 91 92 ```bash package="npm" 93 npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 94 ``` 95 96 ```bash package="yarn" 97 yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 98 ``` 99 100 ```bash package="bun" 101 bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 102 ``` 103 104 If you are using TypeScript, ensure you also upgrade `@types/react` and `@types/react-dom` to their latest versions. 105 106 ## Node.js runtime and browser support 107 108 | Requirement | Change / Details | 109 | ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ | 110 | Node.js 20.9+ | Minimum version now `20.9.0` (LTS); Node.js 18 no longer supported | 111 | TypeScript 5+ | Minimum version now `5.1.0` | 112 | Browsers | Chrome 111+, Edge 111+, Firefox 111+, Safari 16.4+ | 113 114 ## Turbopack by default 115 116 Starting with **Next.js 16**, Turbopack is stable and used by default with `next dev` and `next build` 117 118 Previously you had to enable Turbopack using `--turbopack`, or `--turbo`. 119 120 ```json filename="package.json" 121 { 122 "scripts": { 123 "dev": "next dev --turbopack", 124 "build": "next build --turbopack", 125 "start": "next start" 126 } 127 } 128 ``` 129 130 This is no longer necessary. You can update your `package.json` scripts: 131 132 ```json filename="package.json" 133 { 134 "scripts": { 135 "dev": "next dev", 136 "build": "next build", 137 "start": "next start" 138 } 139 } 140 ``` 141 142 If your project has a [custom `webpack`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/webpack) configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will **fail** to prevent misconfiguration issues. 143 144 You have a few different ways to address this: 145 146 - **Use Turbopack anyway:** Run with `next build --turbopack` to build using Turbopack and ignore your `webpack` config. 147 - **Switch to Turbopack fully:** Migrate your `webpack` config to Turbopack-compatible options. 148 - **Keep using Webpack:** Use the `--webpack` flag to opt out of Turbopack and build with Webpack. 149 150 > **Good to know**: If you see failing builds because a `webpack` configuration was found, but you don't define one yourself, it is likely that a plugin is adding a `webpack` option 151 152 ### Opting out of Turbopack 153 154 If you need to continue using Webpack, you can opt out with the `--webpack` flag. For example, to use Turbopack in development but Webpack for production builds: 155 156 ```json filename="package.json" 157 { 158 "scripts": { 159 "dev": "next dev", 160 "build": "next build --webpack", 161 "start": "next start" 162 } 163 } 164 ``` 165 166 We recommend using Turbopack for development and production. Submit a comment to this [thread](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/77721), if you are unable to switch to Turbopack. 167 168 ### Turbopack configuration location 169 170 The `experimental.turbopack` configuration is out of experimental. 171 172 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" 173 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 174 175 // Next.js 15 - experimental.turbopack 176 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 177 experimental: { 178 turbopack: { 179 // options 180 }, 181 }, 182 } 183 184 export default nextConfig 185 ``` 186 187 You can use it as a top-level `turbopack` option: 188 189 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" 190 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 191 192 // Next.js 16 - turbopack at the top level of nextConfig 193 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 194 turbopack: { 195 // options 196 }, 197 } 198 199 export default nextConfig 200 ``` 201 202 Make sure to review the `Turbopack` configuration [options](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack). **Next.js 16** introduces various improvements and new options, for example: 203 204 - [Advanced Webpack loader conditions](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack#advanced-webpack-loader-conditions) 205 - [debugIds](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack#debug-ids) 206 207 ### Resolve alias fallback 208 209 In some projects, client-side code may import files containing Node.js native modules. This will cause `Module not found: Can't resolve 'fs'` type of errors. 210 211 When this happens, you should refactor your code so that your client-side bundles do not reference these Node.js native modules. 212 213 However, in some cases, this might not be possible. In Webpack the `resolve.fallback` option was typically used to **silence** the error. Turbopack offers a similar option, using `turbopack.resolveAlias`. In this case, tell Turbopack to load an empty module when `fs` is requested for the browser. 214 215 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" 216 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 217 218 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 219 turbopack: { 220 resolveAlias: { 221 fs: { 222 browser: './empty.ts', // We recommend to fix code imports before using this method 223 }, 224 }, 225 }, 226 } 227 228 export default nextConfig 229 ``` 230 231 It is preferable to refactor your modules so that client code doesn't ever import from modules using Node.js native modules. 232 233 ### Sass node_modules imports 234 235 Turbopack fully supports importing Sass files from `node_modules`. Note that while Webpack allowed the legacy tilde (`~`) prefix, Turbopack does not support this syntax. 236 237 In Webpack: 238 239 ```scss filename="styles/globals.scss" 240 @import '~bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css'; 241 ``` 242 243 In Turbopack: 244 245 ```scss filename="styles/globals.scss" 246 @import 'bootstrap/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css'; 247 ``` 248 249 If changing the imports is not possible, you can use `turbopack.resolveAlias`. For example: 250 251 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" 252 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 253 254 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 255 turbopack: { 256 resolveAlias: { 257 '~*': '*', 258 }, 259 }, 260 } 261 262 export default nextConfig 263 ``` 264 265 ### Turbopack File System Caching (beta) 266 267 Turbopack now supports filesystem caching in development, storing compiler artifacts on disk between runs for significantly faster compile times across restarts. 268 269 Enable filesystem caching in your configuration: 270 271 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 272 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 273 274 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 275 experimental: { 276 turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true, 277 }, 278 } 279 280 export default nextConfig 281 ``` 282 283 ```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 284 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 285 const nextConfig = { 286 experimental: { 287 turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true, 288 }, 289 } 290 291 module.exports = nextConfig 292 ``` 293 294 ## Async Request APIs (Breaking change) 295 296 Version 15 introduced [Async Request APIs](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15#async-request-apis-breaking-change) as a breaking change, with **temporary** synchronous compatibility. 297 298 Starting with **Next.js 16**, synchronous access is fully removed. These APIs can only be accessed asynchronously. 299 300 - [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies) 301 - [`headers`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) 302 - [`draftMode`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/draft-mode) 303 - `params` in [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), [`route.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route), [`default.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/default), [`opengraph-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#opengraph-image), [`twitter-image`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image#twitter-image), [`icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#icon), and [`apple-icon`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons#apple-icon). 304 - `searchParams` in [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) 305 306 Use the [codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#migrate-to-async-dynamic-apis) to migrate to async Request-time APIs. 307 308 ### Migrating types for async Request-time APIs 309 310 To help migrate to async `params` and `searchParams`, you can run [`npx next typegen`](/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next#next-typegen-options) to automatically generate these globally available types helpers: 311 312 - [`PageProps`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper) 313 - [`LayoutProps`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper) 314 - [`RouteContext`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) 315 316 > **Good to know**: `typegen` was introduced in Next.js 15.5 317 318 This simplifies type-safe migration to the new async API pattern, and enables you to update your components with full type safety, for example: 319 320 ```tsx filename="/app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 321 export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 322 const { slug } = await props.params 323 const query = await props.searchParams 324 return

Blog Post: {slug}

325 } 326 ``` 327 328 This approach gives you fully type-safe access to `props.params`, including the `slug`, and to `searchParams`, directly within your page. 329 330 ## Async parameters for icon, and open-graph Image (Breaking change) 331 332 > The props passed to the image generating functions in `opengraph-image`, `twitter-image`, `icon`, and `apple-icon`, are now Promises. 333 334 In previous versions, both the `Image` (image generation function), and the `generateImageMetadata` received a `params` object. The `id` returned by `generateImageMetadata` was passed as a string to the image generation function. 335 336 ```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js" 337 // Next.js 15 - synchronous params access 338 export function generateImageMetadata({ params }) { 339 const { slug } = params 340 return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }] 341 } 342 343 // Next.js 15 - synchronous params and id access 344 export default function Image({ params, id }) { 345 const slug = params.slug 346 const imageId = id // string 347 // ... 348 } 349 ``` 350 351 Starting with **Next.js 16**, to align with the [Async Request APIs](#async-request-apis-breaking-change) change, the image generating function now receives `params` and `id` as promises. The `generateImageMetadata` function continues to receive synchronous `params`. 352 353 ```js filename="app/shop/[slug]/opengraph-image.js" 354 export async function generateImageMetadata({ params }) { 355 const { slug } = params 356 return [{ id: '1' }, { id: '2' }] 357 } 358 359 // Next.js 16 - asynchronous params and id access 360 export default async function Image({ params, id }) { 361 const { slug } = await params // params now async 362 const imageId = await id // id is now Promise when using generateImageMetadata 363 // ... 364 } 365 ``` 366 367 ## Async `id` parameter for `sitemap` (Breaking change) 368 369 Previously, the `id` values returned from [`generateSitemaps`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-sitemaps) were passed directly to the `sitemap` generating function. 370 371 ```js filename="app/product/sitemap.js" 372 export async function generateSitemaps() { 373 return [{ id: 0 }, { id: 1 }, { id: 2 }, { id: 3 }] 374 } 375 376 // Next.js 15 - synchronous id access 377 export default async function sitemap({ id }) { 378 const start = id * 50000 // id is a number 379 // ... 380 } 381 ``` 382 383 Starting with **Next.js 16**, the `sitemap` generating function now receives `id` as a promise. 384 385 ```js filename="app/product/sitemap.js" 386 export async function generateSitemaps() { 387 return [{ id: 0 }, { id: 1 }, { id: 2 }, { id: 3 }] 388 } 389 390 // Next.js 16 - asynchronous id access 391 export default async function sitemap({ id }) { 392 const resolvedId = await id // id is now Promise 393 const start = Number(resolvedId) * 50000 394 // ... 395 } 396 ``` 397 398 ## React 19.2 399 400 The App Router in **Next.js 16** uses the latest React [Canary release](https://react.dev/blog/2023/05/03/react-canaries), which includes the newly released React 19.2 features and other features being incrementally stabilized. Highlights include: 401 402 - **[View Transitions](https://react.dev/reference/react/ViewTransition)**: Animate elements that update inside a Transition or navigation 403 - **[`useEffectEvent`](https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffectEvent)**: Extract non-reactive logic from Effects into reusable Effect Event functions 404 - **[Activity](https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity)**: Render "background activity" by hiding UI with `display: none` while maintaining state and cleaning up Effects 405 406 Learn more in the [React 19.2 announcement](https://react.dev/blog/2025/10/01/react-19-2). 407 408 ## React Compiler Support 409 410 Built-in support for the React Compiler is now stable in **Next.js 16** following the React Compiler's 1.0 release. The React Compiler automatically memoizes components, reducing unnecessary re-renders with zero manual code changes. 411 412 The `reactCompiler` configuration option has been promoted from `experimental` to stable. It is not enabled by default as we continue gathering build performance data across different application types. 413 414 ```ts filename="next.config.ts" switcher 415 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 416 417 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 418 reactCompiler: true, 419 } 420 421 export default nextConfig 422 ``` 423 424 ```js filename="next.config.js" switcher 425 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 426 const nextConfig = { 427 reactCompiler: true, 428 } 429 430 module.exports = nextConfig 431 ``` 432 433 Install the latest version of the React Compiler plugin: 434 435 ```bash package="pnpm" 436 pnpm add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 437 ``` 438 439 ```bash package="npm" 440 npm install -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 441 ``` 442 443 ```bash package="yarn" 444 yarn add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 445 ``` 446 447 ```bash package="bun" 448 bun add -D babel-plugin-react-compiler 449 ``` 450 451 > **Good to know:** Expect compile times in development and during builds to be higher when enabling this option as the React Compiler relies on Babel. 452 453 ## Caching APIs 454 455 ### revalidateTag 456 457 [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag) now requires a second argument specifying a [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife#reference) profile. The single-argument form is deprecated and will produce a TypeScript error. 458 459 ```ts 460 // Before 461 revalidateTag('posts') 462 463 // After 464 revalidateTag('posts', 'max') 465 ``` 466 467 If you need immediate expiration rather than stale-while-revalidate, use [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) in Server Actions instead. 468 469 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 470 'use server' 471 472 import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 473 474 export async function updateArticle(articleId: string) { 475 // Mark article data as stale - article readers see stale data while it revalidates 476 revalidateTag(`article-${articleId}`, 'max') 477 } 478 ``` 479 480 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 481 'use server' 482 483 import { revalidateTag } from 'next/cache' 484 485 export async function updateArticle(articleId) { 486 // Mark article data as stale - article readers see stale data while it revalidates 487 revalidateTag(`article-${articleId}`, 'max') 488 } 489 ``` 490 491 Use `revalidateTag` for content where a slight delay in updates is acceptable, such as blog posts, product catalogs, or documentation. Users receive stale content while fresh data loads in the background. 492 493 ### updateTag 494 495 [`updateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag) is a new [Server Actions](/docs/app/getting-started/mutating-data#what-are-server-functions)-only API that provides **read-your-writes** semantics, where a user makes a change and the UI immediately shows the change, rather than stale data. 496 497 It does this by expiring and immediately refreshing data within the same request. 498 499 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 500 'use server' 501 502 import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' 503 504 export async function updateUserProfile(userId: string, profile: Profile) { 505 await db.users.update(userId, profile) 506 507 // Expire cache and refresh immediately - user sees their changes right away 508 updateTag(`user-${userId}`) 509 } 510 ``` 511 512 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 513 'use server' 514 515 import { updateTag } from 'next/cache' 516 517 export async function updateUserProfile(userId, profile) { 518 await db.users.update(userId, profile) 519 520 // Expire cache and refresh immediately - user sees their changes right away 521 updateTag(`user-${userId}`) 522 } 523 ``` 524 525 This ensures interactive features reflect changes immediately. Perfect for forms, user settings, and any workflow where users expect to see their updates instantly. 526 527 Learn more about when to use `updateTag` or `revalidateTag` [here](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag#when-to-use-updatetag). 528 529 ### refresh 530 531 [`refresh`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/refresh) allows you to refresh the client router from within a Server Action. 532 533 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 534 'use server' 535 536 import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 537 538 export async function markNotificationAsRead(notificationId: string) { 539 // Update the notification in the database 540 await db.notifications.markAsRead(notificationId) 541 542 // Refresh the notification count displayed in the header 543 refresh() 544 } 545 ``` 546 547 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 548 'use server' 549 550 import { refresh } from 'next/cache' 551 552 export async function markNotificationAsRead(notificationId) { 553 // Update the notification in the database 554 await db.notifications.markAsRead(notificationId) 555 556 // Refresh the notification count displayed in the header 557 refresh() 558 } 559 ``` 560 561 Use it when you need to refresh the client router after performing an action. 562 563 ### cacheLife and cacheTag 564 565 [`cacheLife`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife) and [`cacheTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag) are now stable. The `unstable_` prefix is no longer needed. 566 567 Wherever you had aliased imports like: 568 569 ```ts 570 import { 571 unstable_cacheLife as cacheLife, 572 unstable_cacheTag as cacheTag, 573 } from 'next/cache' 574 ``` 575 576 You can update your imports to: 577 578 ```ts 579 import { cacheLife, cacheTag } from 'next/cache' 580 ``` 581 582 ## Enhanced Routing and Navigation 583 584 **Next.js 16** includes a complete overhaul of the routing and navigation system, making page transitions leaner and faster. This optimizes how Next.js prefetches and caches navigation data: 585 586 - **Layout deduplication**: When prefetching multiple URLs with a shared layout, the layout is downloaded once. 587 - **Incremental prefetching**: Next.js only prefetches parts not already in cache, rather than entire pages. 588 589 These changes require **no code modifications** and are designed to improve performance across all apps. 590 591 However, you may see more individual prefetch requests with much lower total transfer sizes. We believe this is the right trade-off for nearly all applications. 592 593 If the increased request count causes issues, please let us know by creating an [issue](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues) or [discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions) item. 594 595 ## Partial Prerendering (PPR) 596 597 **Next.js 16** removes the experimental **Partial Prerendering (PPR)** flag and configuration options, including the route level segment `experimental_ppr`. 598 599 Starting with **Next.js 16**, you can opt into PPR using the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) configuration. 600 601 ```js filename="next.config.js" 602 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 603 const nextConfig = { 604 cacheComponents: true, 605 } 606 607 module.exports = nextConfig 608 ``` 609 610 PPR in **Next.js 16** works differently than in **Next.js 15** canaries. If you are using PPR today, stay in the current Next.js 15 canary you are using. See [Migrating to Cache Components](/docs/app/guides/migrating-to-cache-components) for migration patterns. 611 612 ```js filename="next.config.js" 613 /** @type {import('next').NextConfig} */ 614 const nextConfig = { 615 // If you are using PPR today 616 // stay in the current Next.js 15 canary 617 experimental: { 618 ppr: true, 619 }, 620 } 621 622 module.exports = nextConfig 623 ``` 624 625 ## `middleware` to `proxy` 626 627 The `middleware` filename is deprecated, and has been renamed to `proxy` to clarify network boundary and routing focus. 628 629 The `edge` runtime is **NOT** supported in `proxy`. The `proxy` runtime is `nodejs`, and it cannot be configured. If you want to continue using the `edge` runtime, keep using `middleware`. We will follow up on a minor release with further `edge` runtime instructions. 630 631 ```bash filename="Terminal" 632 # Rename your middleware file 633 mv middleware.ts proxy.ts 634 # or 635 mv middleware.js proxy.js 636 ``` 637 638 The named export `middleware` is also deprecated. Rename your function to `proxy`. 639 640 ```ts filename="proxy.ts" switcher 641 export function proxy(request: Request) {} 642 ``` 643 644 ```js filename="proxy.js" switcher 645 export function proxy(request) {} 646 ``` 647 648 We recommend changing the function name to `proxy`, even if you are using a default export. 649 650 Configuration flags that contained the `middleware` name are also renamed.
